items.summary: Война США и Израиля с Ираном должна была взорвать энергетические рынки, но этого еще не произошло. В этом еженедельном документальном фильме Bloomberg Originals объясняется, почему.
items.original_full_text: The US-Israel war with Iran was supposed to blow up energy markets, but that hasn’t happened yet. In this weekly documentary, Bloomberg Originals explains why.

items.title: Today, Ukraine’s new Government, headed by Sergii Koretskyi, began its work. His strength lies specifically in his work at state-owned energy companies. He came to Ukrnafta and changed the company’s processes after oligarchic control. Thanks to Sergii, Ukrnafta began truly working for our state. Naftogaz made it through a difficult winter, and despite all the Russian strikes, Ukraine remained supplied with gas. It is essential to be fully prepared for the next winter. Of course, we are investing as much as possible in Ukraine’s long-range capabilities and in our mid-range strikes to make it as difficult as possible for Russia to expand its offensive against Ukraine and drag out this war. But whatever the scenario, Ukraine must be fully prepared to protect lives and its people and ensure that we make it through the winter. This is the main shared Ukrainian task for the new Government. There will also be changes in our work with partners – agreements should be implemented faster.

​Куча лакун: об утечке данных об АЭС "Куданкулам"
items.original_title: ​Wealth of lacunae : on the Kudankulam nuclear plant data leak
items.summary: Атака программы-вымогателя против подрядчика, участвующего в проекте атомной электростанции «Куданкулам», вызывает обеспокоенность, даже несмотря на то, что ничего, угрожающего целостности станции, не было украдено. В 2019 году вредоносное ПО было обнаружено в административной сети того же объекта, но в NPCIL утверждают, что сеть действующего реактора не пострадала. Новый инцидент продолжает ту же тему. Режим раскрытия информации о нарушениях в Индии непоследователен и зачастую совершенно непрозрачен. Пострадавшие организации склонны полагать, что признание нарушения нанесет ущерб общественному доверию, ценам акций, контрактам и привлечет внимание регулирующих органов. Поэтому они склонны смягчать свои выражения в публичных заявлениях и избегать раскрытия информации до тех пор, пока их не принудят. Многим организациям также не хватает зрелых возможностей реагирования на инциденты, что нередко связано с тем, что они рассматривают кибербезопасность как вопрос соответствия, а не как необходимость. Таким образом, оценить, какие данные пострадали на ранних стадиях атаки, становится технически невозможно. Согласно публичной информации, основная инфраструктура объекта не пострадала; вместо этого группа под названием «World Leaks» организовала атаку с использованием программы-вымогателя, ставящую под угрозу системы, принадлежащие Reliance Infrastructure, одному из инженерных подрядчиков энергоблоков 3 и 4. Данные об инциденте были размещены на сервере Yotta Data Services, который зая
items.original_full_text:
The ransomware attack against a contractor involved in the Kudankulam nuclear power project is concerning, even if nothing threatening the plant’s integrity was stolen. In 2019, malware was found on the same facility’s administrative network, but the NPCIL maintained that the operational reactor network was unaffected. The new incident extends the same theme. India’s breach disclosure regime is inconsistent and often plainly opaque. Affected organisations tend to believe admitting a breach will damage public confidence, share prices, contracts, and invite regulatory scrutiny. So, they tend to ease their language in public statements and avoid disclosure until compelled. Many organisations also lack mature incident response capabilities, not uncommonly because they treat cybersecurity as a matter of compliance rather than necessity. So, assessing what data has been affected in the early stages of an attack becomes technically impossible. According to public information, the facility’s core infrastructure is unaffected; instead, a group called ‘World Leaks’ mounted a ransomware attack compromising systems belonging to Reliance Infrastructure, one of the engineering contractors of Units 3 and 4. The data in the incident were hosted by Yotta Data Services, which said it detected suspicious activity on its servers on May 29.

According to open-source intelligence platform RansomLook, the data began appearing on World Leaks on June 11. However, the NPCIL issued a formal clarification only on July 15, following widespread media reports. Some 14.3 GB of files have been released, including the layouts of ventilation systems, floor plans of an alleged “control room”, supplier and vendor lists, and insurance paperwork. While the files have not been independently authenticated, the actors and their incentives merit a closer look. The NPCIL has said that the files only pertain to infrastructure beyond the facility’s nuclear island. However, such information can still serve so-called intelligence preparation activities. India is the third-most breached country and has already brooked similar attacks against AIIMS Delhi, airlines, and State government portals. In this milieu, the government has positioned Kudankulam as the centrepiece of India’s nuclear power ambitions. As CERT-In is conducting an investigation and Reliance and Yotta have shared their findings with the government, CERT-In and NPCIL should also clarify the nature and authenticity of the files, whether data were exfiltrated before detection, and whether any credentials or supplier accounts have been exposed. Radical transparency is impossible here but basic cyber-hygiene and proactive communication are non-negotiable.

items.title: Анализ: Теневая война России переходит от угрозы к подготовке
items.original_title: Analysis : Russia shadow war is moving from threat to preparation
items.summary:
Литва предупреждает, что Россия может готовить атаки на критически важную инфраструктуру.

Его не следует рассматривать как отдельный изолированный сигнал тревоги. Это соответствует более широкой схеме: Москва исследует восточный фланг НАТО посредством кибер-вторжений, планирования диверсий, давления на море, вмешательства в систему GPS, вербовки прокси-серверов и сомнительных операций, призванных оставаться ниже формального порога войны.

Главный вопрос больше не в том, ведет ли Россия гибридную кампанию против Европы. Это то, как далеко может зайти эта кампания, прежде чем НАТО решит, что она перешла на территорию коллективной обороны.

Наиболее важным моментом является то, что сообщаемые разведывательные данные, судя по всему, не указывают на конкретную цель, место или график. Это имеет значение. Это предполагает, что Россия, возможно, готовит варианты, а не осуществляет неизбежное крупномасштабное нападение.

С точки зрения разведки это различие имеет решающее значение. Подготовка может включать разведку энергетических объектов, кибердоступ к транспортным сетям, наем местных посредников, картирование портовых операций, подмену GPS или тестовые запуски с использованием криминальных посредников. Ничто из этого не требует от Москвы принятия окончательного решения. Но все это дает Москве возможность действовать быстро, если появится политический заказ.

Вероятные цели очевидны: энергетические соединения, железнодорожные коридоры, порты, телекоммуникационные системы, объекты С
items.original_full_text:
Lithuania is warning that Russia may be preparing attacks on critical infrastructure.

It should not be read as a single isolated alarm. It fits a broader pattern: Moscow is probing NATO’s eastern flank through cyber intrusions, sabotage planning, maritime pressure, GPS interference, proxy recruitment and deniable operations designed to stay below the formal threshold of war.

The core question is no longer whether Russia is waging a hybrid campaign against Europe. It is how far that campaign can go before NATO decides it has crossed into collective-defense territory.

The most important point is that the reported intelligence does not appear to identify a specific target, location or timetable. That matters. It suggests Russia may be preparing options rather than executing an imminent large-scale attack.

In intelligence terms, that distinction is critical. Preparation can include reconnaissance of energy sites, cyber access to transport networks, recruitment of local facilitators, mapping of port operations, GPS spoofing or test runs using criminal intermediaries. None of that requires Moscow to make a final decision. But all of it gives Moscow the ability to act quickly if the political order comes.

The likely targets are obvious: energy interconnectors, rail corridors, ports, telecommunications systems, LNG facilities and undersea infrastructure in the Baltics and Poland. These are not symbolic targets. They are the connective tissue of NATO reinforcement and European resilience.

Disrupt a rail node, damage a power cable, compromise a port logistics system or interfere with navigation signals, and the effect is broader than the physical damage. It creates uncertainty, raises insurance and security costs, slows military mobility and pressures governments to prove they can protect civilian life.

Russia’s advantage is deniability. A cyberattack can be blamed on criminals. A fire can look like an accident. A damaged cable can be attributed to a careless ship. A recruited courier or petty criminal can be discarded after the operation. It’s the logic of hybrid warfare: create real damage while forcing the target to argue over attribution, legal thresholds and proportional response.

That’s why Europe’s recent public attribution campaign matters. The UK and EU are not just sanctioning Russian cyber and hybrid operators to punish past behavior. They are trying to strip away deniability before the next incident. Naming officers, companies and networks makes it harder for Moscow to hide behind proxies and front structures. It also warns potential collaborators that the relationship may become public.

For NATO, the hardest problem is not military capability. It is decision speed. Article 5 was designed for an armed attack, not a suspicious ship near a cable, a rail fire, a cyber intrusion and a disinformation burst happening at the same time. Russia understands that ambiguity buys time.

The strategic lesson is blunt: Europe’s front line is no longer only in Ukraine. It runs through substations, ports, rail yards, fiber-optic cables, shipping lanes and data systems. Russia is building a battlespace inside civilian infrastructure. The attack may not have started yet. The preparation almost certainly has.

items.title: Китай получает больше, чем удовольствие от ракетных испытаний
items.original_title: China gets more than a kick out of missile tests
items.summary:
Бонни Юших Ляо 廖雨詩

Добавить TT в качестве предпочтительного источника

Запуск Китаем межконтинентальной баллистической ракеты с атомной подводной лодки в Тихий океан в понедельник на прошлой неделе сразу привлек международное внимание. Это было первое в своем роде публично признанное испытание стратегической ракеты с подводной лодки.

Ракета доминировала в заголовках газет, но смысл, стоящий за ней, имел гораздо большее значение.

Ракета была посланием.

Большая часть дискуссий была сосредоточена на самой ракете — ее дальности, стартовой платформе и расширяющемся ядерном арсенале Китая. Эти вопросы имеют значение, но они упускают из виду более широкую историю. Запуск сигнализировал о том, что Пекин больше не считает, что его стратегические интересы ограничиваются водами, окружающими Китай. Они все больше распространяются на более широкий Индо-Тихоокеанский регион.

В течение многих лет военная модернизация Китая рассматривалась в основном через призму запрета доступа и запрета территорий: удержание иностранных сил вдали от береговой линии Китая и одновременное ужесточение контроля над близлежащими морями. Это объяснение по-прежнему актуально, но его уже недостаточно.

Сегодняшний флот Народно-освободительной армии создан для гораздо большего, чем просто береговая оборона. Авианосцы, подводные лодки дальнего действия, зарубежная логистика и средства ядерного сдерживания морского базирования представляют собой силы, предназначенные для регулярных действий за пределами морской
items.original_full_text:
By Bonnie Yushih Liao 廖雨詩

Add TT as Preferred Source

China’s launch of an intercontinental ballistic missile from a nuclear-powered submarine into the Pacific on Monday last week immediately drew international attention. It was the first publicly acknowledged submarine-launched strategic missile test of its kind.

The missile dominated the headlines, but the message behind it mattered far more.

The missile was the message.

Much of the discussion has focused on the missile itself — its range, launch platform and China’s expanding nuclear arsenal. Those questions matter, but they overlook the larger story. The launch signaled that Beijing no longer sees its strategic interests as confined to the waters surrounding China. They increasingly extend across the wider Indo-Pacific region.

For years, China’s military modernization was viewed mainly through the lens of anti-access and area denial: keeping foreign forces away from China’s coastline while tightening control over nearby seas. That explanation still holds, but it is no longer sufficient.

Today’s People’s Liberation Army Navy is built for much more than coastal defense. Aircraft carriers, long-range submarines, overseas logistics and a sea-based nuclear deterrent reflect a force designed to operate routinely beyond China’s maritime periphery. The missile launch revealed this evolving strategic posture by reinforcing Beijing’s confidence to project power across the Pacific. As strategic competition intensifies, it also signaled that the Pacific is no longer a distant frontier beyond China’s security interests, but an increasingly normal operating environment.

Military capability does more than strengthen deterrence; it gradually reshapes the maritime order. Every long-range patrol, deployment and missile test quietly expands what China presents as its legitimate security space. Strategic maps are redrawn not overnight, but through repeated military and political practice until exceptional actions become routine. As these operations normalize, political expectations shift, making them harder to challenge despite their cumulative consequences. This is the essence of gray-zone competition: changing expectations through sustained military activity without crossing the threshold of armed conflict.

Their purpose is not merely to deter. It is to normalize China’s expanding presence and reshape regional expectations.

For Taiwan, the implications extend well beyond the Taiwan Strait. As China’s presence reaches farther into the Pacific, Taiwan’s security is becoming increasingly linked to the wider Indo-Pacific region. Taiwan therefore needs to think beyond cross-strait deterrence. Strengthening deterrence remains essential, but so does closer cooperation with Japan, Australia and other like-minded partners on maritime domain awareness, intelligence sharing and the protection of critical undersea infrastructure.

Taiwan can also contribute more actively to regional resilience by enhancing practical maritime cooperation with democratic partners. Such efforts not only reinforce Taiwan’s own security, but also help preserve a rules-based maritime order across the Indo-Pacific region.

The headlines surrounding this missile test will eventually fade. The strategic thinking behind it will not.

Every new deployment quietly redraws the map long before anyone realizes it has changed.

The real question is no longer how far China’s missiles can travel. It is how much of the Indo-Pacific region Beijing expects others to accept as its normal operating space.
